channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Ocean rift zone saw spreading happen in a sudden burst - Ars Technica
item.description:
1. Ocean rift zone saw spreading happen in a sudden burst  Ars Technica

item.description:

item.title: Earth may survive the sun's death after all, new study suggests - Space

item.title: Proxima Centauri b is our closest known habitable-zone exoplanet, just over four light-years away, and it may be warm enough for liquid water if it has kept an atmosphere. But that “if” is enormous: its red-dwarf star blasts it with extreme radiation strong enough - Space Daily
item.description: Proxima Centauri b is our closest known habitable-zone exoplanet, just over four light-years away, and it may be warm enough for liquid water if it has kept an atmosphere. But that “if” is enormous: its red-dwarf star blasts it with extreme radiation strong enough  Space Daily

item.title: 50 Years Later, NASA's Voyager Probe Is Reaching A Big Milestone For Space Travel - Yahoo
item.description:
1. 50 Years Later, NASA's Voyager Probe Is Reaching A Big Milestone For Space Travel  Yahoo
  2. The Voyager 1 probe, launched in 1977, is now so far from Earth that a radio signal travelling at the speed of light takes over 22 hours to reach it, and it is still sending data back from interstellar space on a power source the size of a car battery  Space Daily
  3. NASA Has Not Launched Anything Faster Than Voyager in 48 Years. Here Is the Real Menu of Machines That Could Finally Beat It  19FortyFive
  4. In 2024, engineers rescued Voyager 1 after it spent months transmitting gibberish, remotely rewriting and relocating pieces of 46-year-old code around a failed memory chip from more than 24 billion kilometres away.  Space Daily
  5. NASA’s Voyager 1 left Earth in 1977; now its signal takes over 22 hours to reach us and it is still sending data  The Times of India

item.title: An Aboriginal Australian people of northern Queensland speak a language with no words for left or right — only for north, south, east, and west — so instead of asking you to move your left foot, they will ask you to move your east foot, and can point due north al - Space Daily
item.description: An Aboriginal Australian people of northern Queensland speak a language with no words for left or right — only for north, south, east, and west — so instead of asking you to move your left foot, they will ask you to move your east foot, and can point due north al  Space Daily

item.title: The Same Unknown Chemical Signature Has Been Detected on Pluto And Titan - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. The Same Unknown Chemical Signature Has Been Detected on Pluto And Titan  ScienceAlert
  2. Mysterious Compound Detected on Pluto and Titan  WIRED
  3. The James Webb Space Telescope has detected the same unexplained absorption signal on the surfaces of Pluto and Titan — two frozen worlds with very different environments but methane-and-nitrogen chemistry in common — and so far, scientists cannot  Space Daily
  4. Science news this week: James Webb telescope finds a never-before-seen substance, China's 'Great Green Wall' grows faster than natural trees, and a Medici murder mystery is solved  Live Science
  5. Both Pluto and Titan Share a Mystery Molecule That’s Never Been Seen Before  Gizmodo

item.title: Asteroid 16 Psyche, a metal-rich world roughly 225 kilometres wide orbiting between Mars and Jupiter, may contain extraordinary amounts of iron and nickel — enough that back-of-the-envelope estimates have put its theoretical metal value at around $10,000 - Space Daily

item.title: We tend to think of Jupiter as permanently giant, but a 2025 study found it used to be twice its current size, with a magnetic field 50 times more powerful, and it is still quietly shrinking by about 2 centimetres every year - Space Daily
item.description: We tend to think of Jupiter as permanently giant, but a 2025 study found it used to be twice its current size, with a magnetic field 50 times more powerful, and it is still quietly shrinking by about 2 centimetres every year  Space Daily
